event_name: Nostalgic Movie Night at North Cobb Library
blurb: North Cobb Library is hosting a free teen movie night on Monday afternoon featuring nostalgic films. If you've got a teenager home for the summer looking for something to do that doesn't involve a screen in their bedroom, this is an easy one to point them toward. Free, low-key, and close. The specific movie isn't listed, but the nostalgic theme suggests something they'll actually recognize. Sometimes the simplest things are the right call.
scoring_notes: East Cobb's mix of families with teens at home makes this a decent fit, even if it's not a marquee event. Free, local, and fills a real summer boredom gap. Lowest-scoring pick here but qualifies as a real event with community utility. Venue wasn't fully confirmed in the candidate data so noted accordingly.

event_name: Naturalist Club: Amphibians of Cobb County
blurb: This one's a little different, in the best way. The Cobb County Naturalist Club is meeting Tuesday afternoon for a deep dive into the amphibians living right here in Cobb County. Frogs, salamanders, the stuff your kids may have poked at near a creek and you couldn't identify. It's free, it runs 90 minutes, and it's the kind of hands-on nature content that actually makes kids excited to go outside. If you've got curious kids at home this summer, or you're just the type who genuinely wants to know more about what lives in your backyard, this is worth the drive to Kennesaw.
scoring_notes: Solid family fit with teens or younger kids home for summer. Unique enough to stand out from typical programming. Free and hyper-local. Not a blockbuster pick but genuinely different and useful for the family-oriented portion of the East Cobb audience.

event_name: AI Learning Series: Ask AI Anything – Getting Started with Chatbots
blurb: Wednesday evening, Cobb County's library system is hosting a free virtual session on getting started with AI chatbots like ChatGPT. It's part of their AI Learning Series and it's genuinely beginner-friendly: how to ask good questions, how to use it for writing and brainstorming, how to stop being intimidated by it. The virtual format means you don't have to go anywhere, just log on from your couch at 6pm. If you or someone in your house has been curious about AI but hasn't known where to start, this is about as low-barrier as it gets.
scoring_notes: Complements the Gemini session on Saturday well. Virtual format removes friction entirely. Strong fit for the educated East Cobb audience, especially empty nesters who may be curious about AI tools. Slightly less unique since it's part of a series, but still solid value.

blurb: If Monday doesn't work, Cobb County is running another community input session on their 2050 Comprehensive Plan on Thursday evening. Same deal: two hours, free, and your chance to weigh in on how this county grows and changes over the next few decades. Housing, transportation, land use, the stuff that affects your property values and your neighborhood's character. Confirm the venue on the Cobb County website before heading out. Two chances this week to make your voice count.
scoring_notes: Same rationale as Meeting #3. Worth including as a second option for readers who miss Monday. Listing both gives the community actual flexibility. Scores identical to #3.

blurb: Cobb County is building out its 2050 Comprehensive Plan, which is basically the long-range blueprint for how growth, housing, transportation, and land use play out over the next 25 years. And they want your input. Meeting #3 is Monday evening, runs about two hours, and is free to attend. For East Cobb homeowners who care about what this county looks like a decade from now, this is one of the more direct ways to actually be heard. Check the Cobb County website for the confirmed venue before you go.
scoring_notes: 78% homeownership rate in East Cobb makes land use and comprehensive planning highly relevant to this audience. Civic participation is a natural fit for this educated, established demographic. Venue TBD is a minor friction point but doesn't disqualify it.

event_name: Cobb County Juneteenth Public Policy Forum
venue: Jim R. Miller Park & Event Center, Marietta
blurb: Juneteenth is Friday, and Cobb County is marking it with something more substantive than a ceremony. The Juneteenth Public Policy Forum at Jim R. Miller Park brings together community leaders for a full day of conversation about issues directly affecting the African American community here in Cobb. It runs 10am to 4pm, it's free, and it's the kind of event that actually moves the needle on things people care about. If you want to understand your county better and hear from the people working to shape it, this is worth showing up for.
scoring_notes: Tied directly to Juneteenth, which gives it strong timing and cultural relevance. Civic-minded East Cobb readers with high education levels tend to engage with policy content. Free and local. Not the highest entertainment value but strong on community relevance and uniqueness for this specific day.

event_name: Learn Gemini in One Hour: Practical AI Tips for Daily Life
blurb: AI is everywhere right now and most people still feel like they're behind. Switzer Library is running a free one-hour session on Saturday afternoon that walks you through Google Gemini in plain, beginner-friendly terms. We're talking everyday stuff: saving time, brainstorming, writing help, image creation. No tech background required. If you've been curious about what all the AI noise is actually about, this is a low-pressure way to find out without falling down a YouTube rabbit hole. One hour, free, and you'll walk out with something you can actually use.
scoring_notes: East Cobb's educated, higher-income demographic skews toward early adoption of useful tech. A free, accessible AI session at a local library hits a real need without any barrier to entry. Strong fit for the 40s-and-up audience that's curious but hasn't dug in yet.

blurb: If there's one thing East Cobb does well, it's a Braves game on a Friday night. The Braves open a series against the Milwaukee Brewers at Truist Park with first pitch at 7:15, and honestly, this is about as good as a summer evening gets around here. Grab the family, grab some friends, or just grab whoever's around. The Battery has plenty of options before and after the game if you want to make a full night of it. Tickets are still available, but a Friday night opener against a solid Brewers squad won't stay quiet for long. Get your seats and go.
scoring_notes: Braves games are practically a civic religion in East Cobb. Strong family and adult appeal, highly accessible, and the Friday night timing is ideal for this audience. Knocked slightly on uniqueness since it's a regular season game, not a special event, but the opener of a series on a Friday gives it enough energy to feature.

blurb: Madison Beer is playing the Roxy on July 1, which lands on a Wednesday but right at the start of a holiday week when a lot of people have already mentally clocked out. The Roxy is one of the better mid-size venues in Atlanta, good sound, good sight lines, and the Battery around it makes for a solid pre-show dinner. Beer has built a real following in the 20-something demographic and the Roxy crowd for this one will have energy. If you've got someone in your household who's been waiting for this tour to come through Atlanta, now is the time to actually buy the tickets instead of waiting and paying double on the secondary market.
scoring_notes: Madison Beer skews younger than the median Perimeter Post reader at 38, but the mix of young professionals in this audience means there's a real segment here. The Roxy is a great venue and the Battery location adds value. Scored lower on demographic fit because the artist's core audience is late teens to mid-20s, which is on the younger end of this newsletter's readership. Still worth including as a pick for the younger professional segment.

blurb: Sewell Mill's MiniCon is back for 2026 and it's exactly what it sounds like: one day, every fandom, no gatekeeping. Games, vendors, food trucks, and workshops fill the cultural center from 10 to 5, and the vibe skews fun and accessible over intimidating and niche. If you've got kids who are into gaming, anime, comics, or really anything with a fan base, this is a Saturday well spent. And honestly, if you're an adult who grew up on any of this stuff, there's no shame in showing up solo either. It's free, it's local, and it's the kind of day that's hard to have a bad time at.
scoring_notes: The young families and younger end of the professional demographic in this audience will find this genuinely useful. It's free, locally produced, and the all-ages format gives it broad reach. The uniqueness score is moderate because fan conventions exist in various forms around Atlanta, but a free, neighborhood-scale version at Sewell Mill is more accessible and less overwhelming than Dragon Con. Good Saturday option.

event_name: Roswell Music Club: Cameron King, Tenor
blurb: Cameron King is one of classical music's most talked-about young tenors right now, and he's performing at Roswell River Landing on Sunday the 21st as part of the Roswell Music Club series. He trained at Indiana University's Jacobs School of Music and has been racking up performance credits that put him well beyond the local circuit. The River Landing is a beautiful outdoor setting on the Chattahoochee, which makes this the rare classical music event that doesn't require a dress code or a serious commitment to the art form. It's a Sunday, the setting does half the work for you, and you'll walk away having heard something genuinely impressive. Keep an eye on the Roswell Music Club page for time and ticket details.
scoring_notes: The highly educated, higher-income Perimeter audience has real overlap with classical and live music fans who want something more elevated than a cover band. The River Landing outdoor setting softens the barrier to entry for people who aren't die-hard classical listeners. Cameron King as a rising star adds a discovery angle, meaning this audience gets to say they saw him early. Time not confirmed is a flag, but the event itself is real and the fit is solid.

event_name: Atlanta Braves vs. St. Louis Cardinals
blurb: The Cardinals are in town for a series at Truist Park, and June 30 is a Tuesday night that actually has a good reason to get off the couch. Truist is one of the better ballpark experiences in the country, the Battery makes the whole area worth showing up early for, and a midweek Braves game in late June hits differently than a packed weekend series. Tickets are easy to find, you won't be fighting half of Atlanta for a parking spot, and the weather in the evenings has finally gotten to a point where sitting outside for three hours isn't a commitment. Grab a group, get there by 6, and do dinner at the Battery before first pitch.
scoring_notes: Braves games are a staple for this audience demographic and Truist Park is close enough to feel local. The Cardinals are a genuine rivalry matchup with national fan base crossover, which adds a small bump to the uniqueness score. Midweek timing is a mild disadvantage but also means better parking, better access, and a more relaxed crowd, which this audience tends to appreciate. The Battery dining scene makes it a full evening rather than just a game.

event_name: National Typewriter Day with Typewriter Tom
venue: Mimms Museum of Technology and Art, Roswell
blurb: National Typewriter Day is June 20, and Typewriter Tom is bringing his collection of iconic machines back to the Mimms Museum for an afternoon called "The Machines That Wrote America." This is not your average Saturday museum visit. Tom has done this before and the collection is genuinely impressive, and the story of how typewriters shaped journalism, literature, and American culture is more interesting than you'd expect. If you've got a soft spot for analog things, a curious kid who has never seen one up close, or a parent who actually used these machines at work, this is a low-key great afternoon. It's in Roswell, it runs until 5, and it's the one thing on your weekend agenda that nobody else will have done.
scoring_notes: The 70% college-educated audience skews toward cultural and intellectually interesting events, which is exactly what this is. The Mimms Museum is small and under the radar, which makes it feel like a genuine discovery rather than a programmed outing. Uniqueness score is high because there is genuinely nowhere else in the region doing this on this date. Family-friendly but also works for the empty nester segment that wants something different on a Saturday afternoon.

event_name: Argentina vs. Austria World Cup Watch Party at Campus 244
venue: The Lawn at Campus 244, 244 Perimeter Center Pkwy NE, Dunwoody
blurb: Argentina, the defending World Cup champions, play Austria on Monday, and Campus 244 is putting a free watch party on the lawn for it. No tickets, no reservation, just show up and watch Messi and company do their thing in the middle of your lunch hour. The Perimeter Center location makes this genuinely convenient if you're working nearby or just want a midday excuse to be outside. It's casual, it's free, and Argentina vs. Austria is not a throwaway group stage match. If you've been looking for an easy way to plug into the World Cup energy this summer without committing to a full night out, this is it.
scoring_notes: Campus 244 sits directly in the Perimeter audience's backyard, which is a strong geographic fit. Free entry, outdoor setting, and a marquee World Cup matchup make this easy to recommend. The young professional segment of this audience is very likely to follow international soccer. Midday Monday timing is the one soft spot but it's summer, schedules are looser, and the convenience of the location offsets it.

event_name: World Cup Weekend Watch Party at Zanzibar ATL
venue: Zanzibar ATL, 350 Northridge Rd, Sandy Springs
blurb: Atlanta is one of the host cities for the 2026 World Cup, and the energy around it this summer is real. Zanzibar is leaning all the way in with an elevated watch party experience that pairs live soccer with brunch vibes, after-party energy, and the kind of crowd that actually knows what they're watching. This isn't a bar with a game on in the background. It's a full production, and it lands on June 19, which means you've got a Friday night and a reason to go out already built in. Sandy Springs is right in your backyard, so there's no excuse not to check it out. Get there, find a seat, and enjoy the fact that the world just showed up in your city.
scoring_notes: The 2026 World Cup is a once-every-four-years event and Atlanta hosting makes local watch parties genuinely special rather than just routine bar sports nights. The Zanzibar venue skews adult and upscale, which matches the young professional and empty nester mix here. Friday evening timing is ideal. The $105K median income audience won't blink at a cover or drink minimums.
